era_context: The mid-15th century was marked by the final stages of the Hundred Years' War in Western Europe, the consolidation of the Ming Dynasty in China under the Yongle Emperor's successors, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans, and the early Portuguese Age of Exploration along the African coast. In Europe, the Council of Constance's aftermath shaped religious reform movements, while in East Asia, the Zheng He voyages had recently concluded and maritime exploration was being curtailed.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese sources begin to appear)
Interior Americas (no European documentation; Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Battle of Herrings (Journée des Harengs)
events.description: English forces under Sir John Fastolf defeated a French and Scottish relief column attempting to supply the besieged town of Orléans during the Hundred Years' War. The battle is remembered for the English use of defensive tactics and the French failure to break the English supply lines. This engagement demonstrated the continued effectiveness of English archers and disciplined formations, though it was a minor engagement overshadowed by larger campaigns. The victory temporarily secured English supply routes in Normandy.

sources.name: Jean de Wavrin, Recueil des croniques et anchiennes istories de la Grant Bretaigne

events.certainty_note: Date and basic facts confirmed by multiple near-contemporary chronicles, though written decades after the event. The battle occurred on 12 February 1429 in some sources, but 1439 dating appears in certain compilations; this requires verification.
cross_references: Relates to broader Hundred Years' War context (1337–1453)

events.title: Council of Florence (Ecumenical Council)
events.description: The Council of Florence, which began in 1431, continued its work toward reconciliation between the Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church. In 1439, the council achieved a temporary union agreement (Decree of Union) between East and West, signed on 6 July. The agreement addressed the filioque controversy, the use of leavened vs. unleavened bread in the Eucharist, and papal primacy. However, the union proved short-lived and was repudiated by many Orthodox bishops upon their return to Constantinople, particularly after the Ottoman conquest in 1453.
key_figures: Cardinal Bessarion (Orthodox delegate, later Catholic cardinal)

sources.name: Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men

events.certainty_note: The council's proceedings are well-documented in official records and contemporary accounts. The date of the union decree (6 July 1439) is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader religious reform movements of the 15th century

events.title: Portuguese Exploration: Cape Bojador Expeditions
events.region: West African coast (Cape Bojador region, modern Western Sahara)
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1439, Portuguese caravels had made multiple attempts to round Cape Bojador, a psychological and navigational barrier. These expeditions gathered information about African geography, trade routes, and indigenous peoples. The voyages were motivated by desires for gold, spices, and knowledge of African kingdoms, and they marked the beginning of sustained European maritime exploration of Africa.
key_figures: Diogo Gomes (Portuguese explorer, credited with rounding Cape Bojador)

sources.name: Gomes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea
Diogo Gomes, De prima inventione Guinee

events.certainty_note: The exact year of Cape Bojador's first successful rounding is disputed in sources. Some sources place it in 1434, others in 1436 or later. 1439 is assigned here by convention as a representative year of sustained exploration efforts.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1522)
Leads to establishment of trading posts and eventual transatlantic slave trade

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Zheng He's Final Voyages Conclude
events.description: The great treasure voyages of Admiral Zheng He, which had begun in 1405, were winding down by 1439. Zheng He himself died in 1433, but his final voyage (the seventh) returned to China around 1433. By 1439, the Ming court had largely ceased sponsoring large-scale maritime expeditions, redirecting resources to land-based military concerns and the Great Wall. This marked a turning point in global exploration: while Europe was beginning sustained oceanic exploration, China withdrew from it, a decision with profound long-term consequences.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler, died 1424, but his policies continued)

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The conclusion of Zheng He's voyages and the shift in Ming policy are well-documented in official Ming records and contemporary accounts by voyage participants.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming Dynasty consolidation (1368–1644)

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Consolidation in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad II continued its consolidation of power in the Balkans following the Ottoman-Venetian War (1426–1432). By 1439, Ottoman control extended over much of the former Serbian and Bulgarian territories. The Ottomans were establishing administrative systems and military garrisons throughout the region. This period saw the integration of Balkan territories into the Ottoman administrative structure, with the devshirme system (recruitment of Christian boys for military and administrative service) becoming more systematized.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and administrative documents
Ashikpashazade, Tevarih-i Al-i Osman (History of the House of Osman)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1439 are not always precisely dated in sources.
cross_references: Leads to Ottoman conquest of Constantinople (1453-001)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Herrings (1439-001) presents a dating discrepancy: some sources place this battle in 1429 rather than 1439. Modern scholarship generally accepts 1429 as the correct date, making the inclusion of this event under 1439 questionable. The Council of Florence's union decree is sometimes dated to 1438 in certain sources, though 1439 is the more widely accepted date. The conclusion of Zheng He's voyages is sometimes attributed to 1433 rather than 1439, though the broader policy shift away from maritime exploration continued through 1439.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1439 is moderate but unevenly distributed. European sources (particularly Italian, French, and Portuguese) are relatively abundant, while Ottoman sources are more limited in English-language scholarship. Chinese sources are rich but require specialized knowledge to access. African sources are minimal—the Portuguese perspective dominates the exploration narrative. The historical record is shaped by the survival of written chronicles and official records, which tend to emphasize political and military events over economic or social developments. There is a significant Western bias in available English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Florence's union was repudiated after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople, which the union had hoped to prevent
Portuguese exploration of Africa was part of the broader Age of Exploration
Chinese maritime exploration was concluding while European exploration was beginning
Ottoman consolidation in the Balkans was a precursor to the conquest of Constantinople
